channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Rents begin to bounce back after April price drop
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK Rental Market Shows Signs of Cooling Despite Supply Pressures

Recent data indicates that UK rental price growth continues to moderate, with annual inflation reaching its lowest point in nearly a year. According to the latest rental index figures, average rents across England rose by just 1.7% in May compared to the same period last year, marking the second consecutive month of subdued growth. The average monthly rent now stands at £1,211, a marginal increase from £1,191 in May 2023.

Regional Variations in Rental Performance

The rental market is displaying notable regional disparities. London maintains its position as the strongest performer, with rents climbing 5.6% annually to reach £2,194 per month. This growth occurs against a backdrop of constrained housing supply in the capital, where new property development continues to lag behind targets.

Meanwhile, the North East experienced a significant rebound, with rents jumping 5.5% in May following a 4.9% decline in April. Yorkshire and the Humber also showed resilience with a 3.2% monthly increase. However, several regions recorded month-on-month decreases, including:

  • The East Midlands
  • East of England
  • South West
  • North West

Changing Market Dynamics

The current rental landscape presents some unusual seasonal patterns. For the first time since the pandemic, May rents have failed to surpass their March levels, with average prices remaining slightly below the £1,212 peak recorded earlier in the year. This cooling trend persists despite ongoing supply constraints across much of the country.

Market analysts suggest several factors may be contributing to the moderation in rental growth:

  • Changing migration patterns potentially easing demand
  • Improved affordability pressures as wage growth outpaces rent increases
  • Tenants becoming more resistant to significant price hikes

While the rental market remains competitive in many areas, particularly London, the latest figures suggest a potential shift in dynamics. Prospective tenants might find slightly more favourable conditions in some regions, though supply shortages continue to underpin prices across most of the country.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK Rental Market Shows Signs of Cooling Despite Supply Pressures

Recent data indicates that UK rental price growth continues to moderate, with annual inflation reaching its lowest point in nearly a year. According to the latest rental index figures, average rents across England rose by just 1.7% in May compared to the same period last year, marking the second consecutive month of subdued growth. The average monthly rent now stands at £1,211, a marginal increase from £1,191 in May 2023.

Regional Variations in Rental Performance

The rental market is displaying notable regional disparities. London maintains its position as the strongest performer, with rents climbing 5.6% annually to reach £2,194 per month. This growth occurs against a backdrop of constrained housing supply in the capital, where new property development continues to lag behind targets.

Meanwhile, the North East experienced a significant rebound, with rents jumping 5.5% in May following a 4.9% decline in April. Yorkshire and the Humber also showed resilience with a 3.2% monthly increase. However, several regions recorded month-on-month decreases, including:

  • The East Midlands
  • East of England
  • South West
  • North West

Changing Market Dynamics

The current rental landscape presents some unusual seasonal patterns. For the first time since the pandemic, May rents have failed to surpass their March levels, with average prices remaining slightly below the £1,212 peak recorded earlier in the year. This cooling trend persists despite ongoing supply constraints across much of the country.

Market analysts suggest several factors may be contributing to the moderation in rental growth:

  • Changing migration patterns potentially easing demand
  • Improved affordability pressures as wage growth outpaces rent increases
  • Tenants becoming more resistant to significant price hikes

While the rental market remains competitive in many areas, particularly London, the latest figures suggest a potential shift in dynamics. Prospective tenants might find slightly more favourable conditions in some regions, though supply shortages continue to underpin prices across most of the country.

item.title: UK property transactions down 3% in April
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

UK property transactions saw a modest 3% decline in April compared to March, according to the latest figures. Whilst this represents a slight monthly dip, analysts note the housing market continues to demonstrate notable resilience given current economic headwinds. The relatively stable transaction levels suggest sustained demand from both homeowners and investors.

Market shows stability despite economic pressures

The small reduction in property transactions comes against a backdrop of rising interest rates and persistent inflation. Industry observers highlight that the marginal decrease indicates the market is holding up better than some forecasts predicted. Several factors may be contributing to this stability:

  • Strong employment figures supporting buyer confidence
  • Limited housing stock maintaining price stability
  • Lenders offering competitive mortgage products

Regional variations in transaction levels

Whilst national figures show a modest decline, regional performance varies significantly. Some areas of the UK continue to see robust activity, particularly in more affordable markets where first-time buyers remain active. The market appears to be adjusting to higher borrowing costs rather than experiencing a significant downturn.

Those considering entering the property market might wish to research local trends carefully, as conditions can differ markedly between postcodes. Mortgage brokers often highlight the importance of understanding regional dynamics when making property decisions.

With the Bank of England's base rate decisions continuing to influence mortgage pricing, many potential buyers are weighing their options carefully. The coming months will reveal whether April's slight dip represents a temporary fluctuation or the beginning of a more sustained trend.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

UK property transactions saw a modest 3% decline in April compared to March, according to the latest figures. Whilst this represents a slight monthly dip, analysts note the housing market continues to demonstrate notable resilience given current economic headwinds. The relatively stable transaction levels suggest sustained demand from both homeowners and investors.

Market shows stability despite economic pressures

The small reduction in property transactions comes against a backdrop of rising interest rates and persistent inflation. Industry observers highlight that the marginal decrease indicates the market is holding up better than some forecasts predicted. Several factors may be contributing to this stability:

  • Strong employment figures supporting buyer confidence
  • Limited housing stock maintaining price stability
  • Lenders offering competitive mortgage products

Regional variations in transaction levels

Whilst national figures show a modest decline, regional performance varies significantly. Some areas of the UK continue to see robust activity, particularly in more affordable markets where first-time buyers remain active. The market appears to be adjusting to higher borrowing costs rather than experiencing a significant downturn.

Those considering entering the property market might wish to research local trends carefully, as conditions can differ markedly between postcodes. Mortgage brokers often highlight the importance of understanding regional dynamics when making property decisions.

With the Bank of England's base rate decisions continuing to influence mortgage pricing, many potential buyers are weighing their options carefully. The coming months will reveal whether April's slight dip represents a temporary fluctuation or the beginning of a more sustained trend.

item.title: Rental yields stabilise to 6.5% in Q1
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental Yields Hold Steady at 6.5% as Market Shows Resilience

Private rental sector investors may find reassurance in new figures showing stabilised yields during the first quarter of the year. Average rental yields across the UK have settled at 6.5%, suggesting continued profitability for landlords despite broader economic pressures.

Occupancy Rates Underpin Stable Returns

The consistent performance appears linked to sustained tenant demand, with occupancy levels remaining robust across most regions. Property investors might note that this stability comes amidst:

  • Gradual easing of inflation pressures
  • Relatively strong employment figures
  • Ongoing housing supply constraints

Market analysts suggest these factors have collectively helped maintain equilibrium between rental prices and property values, preventing the yield compression some had predicted.

Regional Variations Persist

Whilst the national average provides a helpful benchmark, experienced investors will be aware that significant regional disparities continue. Northern cities have reportedly maintained stronger yields compared to southern counterparts, though London's prime postcodes continue to command premium rents.

Those reviewing their property portfolios might consider how local market conditions could affect their specific investments, particularly regarding:

  • Local employment trends
  • New housing developments
  • Transport infrastructure projects

With the Bank of England's base rate decisions continuing to influence mortgage costs, the stability in rental yields could prove significant for landlords assessing their financing options. As always, professional financial advice remains essential when making investment decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental Yields Hold Steady at 6.5% as Market Shows Resilience

Private rental sector investors may find reassurance in new figures showing stabilised yields during the first quarter of the year. Average rental yields across the UK have settled at 6.5%, suggesting continued profitability for landlords despite broader economic pressures.

Occupancy Rates Underpin Stable Returns

The consistent performance appears linked to sustained tenant demand, with occupancy levels remaining robust across most regions. Property investors might note that this stability comes amidst:

  • Gradual easing of inflation pressures
  • Relatively strong employment figures
  • Ongoing housing supply constraints

Market analysts suggest these factors have collectively helped maintain equilibrium between rental prices and property values, preventing the yield compression some had predicted.

Regional Variations Persist

Whilst the national average provides a helpful benchmark, experienced investors will be aware that significant regional disparities continue. Northern cities have reportedly maintained stronger yields compared to southern counterparts, though London's prime postcodes continue to command premium rents.

Those reviewing their property portfolios might consider how local market conditions could affect their specific investments, particularly regarding:

  • Local employment trends
  • New housing developments
  • Transport infrastructure projects

With the Bank of England's base rate decisions continuing to influence mortgage costs, the stability in rental yields could prove significant for landlords assessing their financing options. As always, professional financial advice remains essential when making investment decisions.

item.title: First Home Fund boost for Scottish housing market
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Scottish housing market has received a potential boost with the relaunch of the First Home Fund, an initiative designed to help first-time buyers secure their initial property purchase. The government-backed scheme offers interest-free loans towards deposit costs, potentially making homeownership more accessible for those struggling to save the traditional upfront amounts.

How the First Home Fund works

The revamped First Home Fund provides eligible buyers in Scotland with up to £25,000 (or 49% of the property value) as an interest-free loan towards their deposit. Unlike some shared equity schemes, participants own 100% of their property from the outset. The loan becomes repayable when the home is sold, with the amount adjusted according to the property's current market value.

Key features of the scheme include:

  • Available to first-time buyers and previous homeowners who haven't owned property in the last three years
  • Can be combined with other Scottish government schemes like LIFT (Low-cost Initiative for First Time Buyers)
  • Minimum personal deposit contribution of £1,000 required
  • Property price cap of £175,000 for most areas

Potential impact on the Scottish property market

Industry observers suggest the scheme's return could stimulate activity in Scotland's first-time buyer market, particularly in areas where property prices remain relatively affordable. The interest-free nature of the loan may prove particularly attractive compared to traditional mortgage products or alternative deposit assistance schemes.

Prospective buyers might wish to consider that:

  • The scheme operates on a first-come, first-served basis with limited funding available
  • Approved lenders may have specific mortgage criteria alongside the scheme requirements
  • Future property value changes will affect the final repayment amount

With housing affordability remaining a challenge across the UK, the First Home Fund represents one of several options available to Scottish buyers exploring routes onto the property ladder. Those considering the scheme may benefit from consulting an independent mortgage adviser to understand how it could fit with their individual circumstances.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Scottish housing market has received a potential boost with the relaunch of the First Home Fund, an initiative designed to help first-time buyers secure their initial property purchase. The government-backed scheme offers interest-free loans towards deposit costs, potentially making homeownership more accessible for those struggling to save the traditional upfront amounts.

How the First Home Fund works

The revamped First Home Fund provides eligible buyers in Scotland with up to £25,000 (or 49% of the property value) as an interest-free loan towards their deposit. Unlike some shared equity schemes, participants own 100% of their property from the outset. The loan becomes repayable when the home is sold, with the amount adjusted according to the property's current market value.

Key features of the scheme include:

  • Available to first-time buyers and previous homeowners who haven't owned property in the last three years
  • Can be combined with other Scottish government schemes like LIFT (Low-cost Initiative for First Time Buyers)
  • Minimum personal deposit contribution of £1,000 required
  • Property price cap of £175,000 for most areas

Potential impact on the Scottish property market

Industry observers suggest the scheme's return could stimulate activity in Scotland's first-time buyer market, particularly in areas where property prices remain relatively affordable. The interest-free nature of the loan may prove particularly attractive compared to traditional mortgage products or alternative deposit assistance schemes.

Prospective buyers might wish to consider that:

  • The scheme operates on a first-come, first-served basis with limited funding available
  • Approved lenders may have specific mortgage criteria alongside the scheme requirements
  • Future property value changes will affect the final repayment amount

With housing affordability remaining a challenge across the UK, the First Home Fund represents one of several options available to Scottish buyers exploring routes onto the property ladder. Those considering the scheme may benefit from consulting an independent mortgage adviser to understand how it could fit with their individual circumstances.

item.title: Seaside investment hotspots revealed
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research highlights several coastal towns across the UK where property prices are surging, offering potential opportunities for investors and homeowners alike. The analysis reveals Bootle in Merseyside leading the pack with an 11% annual increase in average asking prices, followed closely by Crosby in Liverpool and Penarth in South Glamorgan, both recording 9% growth.

Coastal towns with strongest price growth

The study identifies several seaside locations experiencing notable property value increases:

  • Bootle, Merseyside: 11% annual price growth
  • Crosby, Liverpool: 9% increase
  • Penarth, South Glamorgan: 9% rise
  • Llantwit Major, South Glamorgan: 8% uplift
  • Llanelli, Carmarthenshire: 7% growth

These figures suggest a growing appetite for coastal properties, potentially driven by changing lifestyle preferences following the pandemic and the increasing popularity of hybrid working arrangements. The data indicates that buyers are looking beyond traditional city centres for more affordable options with quality-of-life benefits.

Factors driving seaside property demand

Several elements may be contributing to this coastal property boom. Many of these locations offer relatively affordable housing compared to nearby cities, coupled with the appeal of seaside living. Improved transport links and digital infrastructure have also made these areas more accessible for those who split their time between home and office working.

Prospective buyers might consider researching local amenities, transport connections and future development plans when evaluating these markets. It could be worth consulting with local estate agents to understand the specific dynamics of each area, as coastal markets can vary significantly even within the same region.

While these figures show promising growth, it's worth remembering that past performance doesn't guarantee future results. Property values can fluctuate based on numerous economic factors, and thorough research remains essential before making any investment decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research highlights several coastal towns across the UK where property prices are surging, offering potential opportunities for investors and homeowners alike. The analysis reveals Bootle in Merseyside leading the pack with an 11% annual increase in average asking prices, followed closely by Crosby in Liverpool and Penarth in South Glamorgan, both recording 9% growth.

Coastal towns with strongest price growth

The study identifies several seaside locations experiencing notable property value increases:

  • Bootle, Merseyside: 11% annual price growth
  • Crosby, Liverpool: 9% increase
  • Penarth, South Glamorgan: 9% rise
  • Llantwit Major, South Glamorgan: 8% uplift
  • Llanelli, Carmarthenshire: 7% growth

These figures suggest a growing appetite for coastal properties, potentially driven by changing lifestyle preferences following the pandemic and the increasing popularity of hybrid working arrangements. The data indicates that buyers are looking beyond traditional city centres for more affordable options with quality-of-life benefits.

Factors driving seaside property demand

Several elements may be contributing to this coastal property boom. Many of these locations offer relatively affordable housing compared to nearby cities, coupled with the appeal of seaside living. Improved transport links and digital infrastructure have also made these areas more accessible for those who split their time between home and office working.

Prospective buyers might consider researching local amenities, transport connections and future development plans when evaluating these markets. It could be worth consulting with local estate agents to understand the specific dynamics of each area, as coastal markets can vary significantly even within the same region.

While these figures show promising growth, it's worth remembering that past performance doesn't guarantee future results. Property values can fluctuate based on numerous economic factors, and thorough research remains essential before making any investment decisions.

item.title: The benefits of made to measure cupboard doors for B2B projects
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Commercial property owners and developers are increasingly turning to made-to-measure cupboard doors as a way to enhance both functionality and aesthetic appeal in business premises. These bespoke solutions offer distinct advantages over standard off-the-shelf options, particularly when it comes to maximising space efficiency and creating tailored interiors that meet specific operational needs.

Why bespoke storage solutions matter for commercial properties

In commercial settings, every square foot counts. Made-to-measure cupboard doors allow businesses to:

  • Utilise awkward or irregular spaces that standard units wouldn't fit
  • Create seamless storage solutions that complement the overall interior design
  • Improve workflow efficiency with storage tailored to specific business requirements
  • Enhance durability with materials selected for high-traffic environments

For landlords and property investors, these custom solutions can potentially add value to commercial units whilst addressing the practical needs of tenants across various sectors, from hospitality to professional services.

Long-term benefits for property investors

Investing in quality fitted storage solutions could prove advantageous for those managing commercial property portfolios. Well-designed made-to-measure cupboards often:

  • Require less maintenance than standard units due to superior materials and construction
  • Extend the lifespan of storage areas, reducing replacement costs
  • Create a more premium feel that may justify higher rental values
  • Provide flexibility to accommodate different tenant requirements over time

Whilst the initial outlay for bespoke joinery might be higher than mass-produced alternatives, the potential long-term savings and value-add could make it a worthwhile consideration for commercial property owners.

As businesses continue to prioritise efficient use of space and professional presentation, made-to-measure storage solutions are likely to remain an important feature in commercial interior projects. Property investors might consider how these tailored elements could enhance both the functionality and marketability of their assets.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Commercial property owners and developers are increasingly turning to made-to-measure cupboard doors as a way to enhance both functionality and aesthetic appeal in business premises. These bespoke solutions offer distinct advantages over standard off-the-shelf options, particularly when it comes to maximising space efficiency and creating tailored interiors that meet specific operational needs.

Why bespoke storage solutions matter for commercial properties

In commercial settings, every square foot counts. Made-to-measure cupboard doors allow businesses to:

  • Utilise awkward or irregular spaces that standard units wouldn't fit
  • Create seamless storage solutions that complement the overall interior design
  • Improve workflow efficiency with storage tailored to specific business requirements
  • Enhance durability with materials selected for high-traffic environments

For landlords and property investors, these custom solutions can potentially add value to commercial units whilst addressing the practical needs of tenants across various sectors, from hospitality to professional services.

Long-term benefits for property investors

  • Require less maintenance than standard units due to superior materials and construction
  • Extend the lifespan of storage areas, reducing replacement costs
  • Create a more premium feel that may justify higher rental values

Whilst the initial outlay for bespoke joinery might be higher than mass-produced alternatives, the potential long-term savings and value-add could make it a worthwhile consideration for commercial property owners.

As businesses continue to prioritise efficient use of space and professional presentation, made-to-measure storage solutions are likely to remain an important feature in commercial interior projects. Property investors might consider how these tailored elements could enhance both the functionality and marketability of their assets.

item.title: The Hidden Costs of Buying the Wrong Investment Property: 7 Mistakes Investors Make Before They Buy
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Whilst many property investors focus heavily on rental yields and market trends, some of the most expensive mistakes occur during the initial property search and purchase stages. Overlooking key factors early in the process could lead to costly consequences that only become apparent months or years later. Being aware of common pitfalls might help investors make more informed decisions when building their portfolios.

Common Pre-Purchase Oversights

Investors often concentrate on the financial aspects of property investment whilst underestimating other crucial considerations. Some frequent oversights include:

  • Failing to properly research local rental demand and tenant profiles
  • Underestimating ongoing maintenance costs for older properties
  • Overlooking restrictive covenants or unusual lease terms
  • Not accounting for potential void periods in cash flow calculations
  • Ignoring upcoming local developments that could affect property values

These factors might not immediately impact profitability but could significantly affect long-term returns. Investors might wish to create a comprehensive checklist that goes beyond basic financial metrics when evaluating potential purchases.

Hidden Costs That Can Erode Returns

Some expenses only become apparent after completing a purchase. Unexpected costs that could affect an investment's performance include:

  • Higher-than-anticipated renovation costs to make properties lettable
  • Unexpected service charges or ground rent increases in leasehold properties
  • Compliance costs for new energy efficiency regulations
  • Legal fees for dealing with problematic tenancies or disputes
  • Insurance premiums for properties in flood-risk areas

Conducting thorough due diligence, including detailed property surveys and legal checks, might help identify potential issues before exchange. Investors may also consider building larger financial buffers to account for unforeseen expenses.

Property investment requires careful planning beyond simple yield calculations. By paying attention to often-overlooked factors during the purchasing process, investors might avoid costly mistakes and build more resilient portfolios. Professional advice from qualified mortgage brokers and property specialists could provide valuable insights tailored to individual circumstances.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Whilst many property investors focus heavily on rental yields and market trends, some of the most expensive mistakes occur during the initial property search and purchase stages. Overlooking key factors early in the process could lead to costly consequences that only become apparent months or years later. Being aware of common pitfalls might help investors make more informed decisions when building their portfolios.

Common Pre-Purchase Oversights

Investors often concentrate on the financial aspects of property investment whilst underestimating other crucial considerations. Some frequent oversights include:

  • Failing to properly research local rental demand and tenant profiles
  • Underestimating ongoing maintenance costs for older properties
  • Overlooking restrictive covenants or unusual lease terms
  • Not accounting for potential void periods in cash flow calculations
  • Ignoring upcoming local developments that could affect property values

These factors might not immediately impact profitability but could significantly affect long-term returns. Investors might wish to create a comprehensive checklist that goes beyond basic financial metrics when evaluating potential purchases.

Hidden Costs That Can Erode Returns

Some expenses only become apparent after completing a purchase. Unexpected costs that could affect an investment's performance include:

  • Higher-than-anticipated renovation costs to make properties lettable
  • Unexpected service charges or ground rent increases in leasehold properties
  • Compliance costs for new energy efficiency regulations
  • Legal fees for dealing with problematic tenancies or disputes
  • Insurance premiums for properties in flood-risk areas

Conducting thorough due diligence, including detailed property surveys and legal checks, might help identify potential issues before exchange. Investors may also consider building larger financial buffers to account for unforeseen expenses.

Property investment requires careful planning beyond simple yield calculations. By paying attention to often-overlooked factors during the purchasing process, investors might avoid costly mistakes and build more resilient portfolios. Professional advice from qualified mortgage brokers and property specialists could provide valuable insights tailored to individual circumstances.

item.title: Is the landlord right to claim for cleaning at the end of the tenancy?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlord awarded cleaning costs after tenant dispute

A recent tenancy dispute has highlighted the importance of understanding cleaning obligations when moving out of a rental property. The case involved a landlord claiming £245 for professional cleaning after their tenant vacated, arguing the property hadn't been returned to its original condition.

The cleaning standards debate

Cleaning disagreements remain one of the most common issues at the end of tenancies. This particular case saw differing expectations between landlord and tenant regarding what constitutes an acceptable standard. The tenant maintained they'd left the property in a "reasonable domestic condition", while the landlord expected cleanliness matching the professional standard documented at check-in.

The inventory report from the tenancy start showed:

  • Professional cleaning throughout
  • Spotless kitchen appliances
  • Limescale-free bathroom fittings
  • Freshly vacuumed carpets

At check-out, evidence showed grease in the oven, limescale deposits, dusty skirting boards and carpet stains. The tenant argued some issues like limescale were due to hard water rather than inadequate cleaning.

Key lessons for tenants and landlords

The adjudicator's decision to award the landlord £180 (reduced from the original claim) provides several important reminders:

  • The check-in inventory sets the cleanliness benchmark tenants need to match
  • "Domestic cleaning" may not meet contractual obligations if the property was professionally cleaned initially
  • Photographic evidence from both parties strengthens any claim
  • Tenants might consider professional cleaning if unsure about meeting required standards

This case illustrates how clear documentation and understanding of tenancy agreements can help prevent disputes. Both landlords and tenants may wish to ensure proper records are kept and expectations are communicated clearly from the outset.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlord awarded cleaning costs after tenant dispute

A recent tenancy dispute has highlighted the importance of understanding cleaning obligations when moving out of a rental property. The case involved a landlord claiming £245 for professional cleaning after their tenant vacated, arguing the property hadn't been returned to its original condition.

The cleaning standards debate

Cleaning disagreements remain one of the most common issues at the end of tenancies. This particular case saw differing expectations between landlord and tenant regarding what constitutes an acceptable standard. The tenant maintained they'd left the property in a "reasonable domestic condition", while the landlord expected cleanliness matching the professional standard documented at check-in.

The inventory report from the tenancy start showed:

  • Professional cleaning throughout
  • Spotless kitchen appliances
  • Limescale-free bathroom fittings
  • Freshly vacuumed carpets

At check-out, evidence showed grease in the oven, limescale deposits, dusty skirting boards and carpet stains. The tenant argued some issues like limescale were due to hard water rather than inadequate cleaning.

Key lessons for tenants and landlords

The adjudicator's decision to award the landlord £180 (reduced from the original claim) provides several important reminders:

  • The check-in inventory sets the cleanliness benchmark tenants need to match
  • "Domestic cleaning" may not meet contractual obligations if the property was professionally cleaned initially
  • Photographic evidence from both parties strengthens any claim
  • Tenants might consider professional cleaning if unsure about meeting required standards

This case illustrates how clear documentation and understanding of tenancy agreements can help prevent disputes. Both landlords and tenants may wish to ensure proper records are kept and expectations are communicated clearly from the outset.

item.title: Former Knight Frank directors launch new property consultancy
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Two seasoned professionals from Knight Frank’s land and development team have embarked on a new venture, establishing a property consultancy tailored to the Midlands market. Will Jordan and Charlie Steele, who boast a combined experience of nearly four decades in the residential development sector, have launched Ashlawn Chase. Based in Henley-in-Arden, the firm aims to provide expert advice to landowners, developers, and investors on land acquisitions, disposals, and strategic opportunities.

Expertise and Focus

Ashlawn Chase positions itself as a consultancy offering straightforward, commercially focused advice, with clients gaining direct access to its founders. Jordan and Steele bring a wealth of experience, having previously advised on a variety of projects across the Midlands, including city centre regeneration schemes, residential developments, and mixed-use sites. Their roles at Knight Frank involved significant transactions and advisory work in Birmingham and the wider region, equipping them with a deep understanding of the local market.

The new consultancy arrives at a time when the residential land market remains active yet challenging. Developers and landowners are increasingly seeking specialist guidance to navigate planning hurdles, viability pressures, and evolving market conditions. Ashlawn Chase aims to address these needs by providing a personal and agile service, grounded in strong relationships and a results-driven approach.

A Personalised Approach

One of the key principles behind Ashlawn Chase is the emphasis on direct access to experienced advisers. Jordan, formerly head of development at Knight Frank’s Birmingham office, highlighted the importance of understanding both opportunities and risks in the current market. “We saw an opportunity to create a business that offers straightforward, commercially focused advice without unnecessary complexity,” he said. “Clients increasingly value direct access to experienced advisers who can guide them through the challenges.”

Steele echoed this sentiment, noting that their shared approach to business has always been relationship-led and commercially grounded. “Launching Ashlawn Chase gives us the opportunity to provide a more personal and agile service to clients across the region,” he explained. This client-centric philosophy is expected to set the consultancy apart in a competitive market.

As Ashlawn Chase begins its journey, its founders are well-positioned to support Midlands-based clients in navigating the complexities of the residential land market. With their extensive experience and commitment to delivering tailored solutions, Jordan and Steele aim to make a significant impact in the region’s property landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Two seasoned professionals from Knight Frank’s land and development team have embarked on a new venture, establishing a property consultancy tailored to the Midlands market. Will Jordan and Charlie Steele, who boast a combined experience of nearly four decades in the residential development sector, have launched Ashlawn Chase. Based in Henley-in-Arden, the firm aims to provide expert advice to landowners, developers, and investors on land acquisitions, disposals, and strategic opportunities.

Expertise and Focus

Ashlawn Chase positions itself as a consultancy offering straightforward, commercially focused advice, with clients gaining direct access to its founders. Jordan and Steele bring a wealth of experience, having previously advised on a variety of projects across the Midlands, including city centre regeneration schemes, residential developments, and mixed-use sites. Their roles at Knight Frank involved significant transactions and advisory work in Birmingham and the wider region, equipping them with a deep understanding of the local market.

The new consultancy arrives at a time when the residential land market remains active yet challenging. Developers and landowners are increasingly seeking specialist guidance to navigate planning hurdles, viability pressures, and evolving market conditions. Ashlawn Chase aims to address these needs by providing a personal and agile service, grounded in strong relationships and a results-driven approach.

One of the key principles behind Ashlawn Chase is the emphasis on direct access to experienced advisers.
